Computer Vision · Forest Monitoring

Quantifying
Forest Canopy
from the Sky

A drone-assisted computer vision system for accurate, scalable tree counting in regional landscapes — transforming raw aerial imagery into actionable environmental intelligence.

DomainComputer Vision
MethodCanny Edge Detection
Accuracy95%
CANNY EDGE DETECTION · CAMPUS DATASET
Detected
16 Trees
Overview

The Problem

Traditional tree counting is slow, labor-intensive, and error-prone — especially in challenging terrain.

Keywords
Remote SensingUAV / DroneOpenCV Edge DetectionImage ProcessingForest Canopy Computer VisionGDALPython

Forests are the lungs of our planet — yet monitoring their health at scale remains one of the most persistent challenges in environmental science. Accurately determining the number of trees in a specific region is crucial for assessing biodiversity, tracking deforestation, guiding urban planning, and managing natural resources.

"This approach has the potential to transform the way we monitor forests — with far-reaching implications for environmental conservation."

This project presents a novel, automated tree-counting pipeline that leverages high-resolution drone imagery combined with classical computer vision techniques. The system processes aerial TIFF images through a carefully engineered sequence: grayscale conversion, Gaussian blur, Canny edge detection, and morphological analysis — detecting and counting individual tree crowns with remarkable accuracy.

Validated on two real-world datasets captured over campuses in Vijayawada, the Canny Edge Detection approach achieved 95% accuracy — outperforming Watershed Segmentation, Adaptive Thresholding, and Region-based Segmentation. The result is a fully functional GUI application accessible to non-technical users.

How It Works

The Methodology

Four tightly integrated modules transform a raw drone image into a precise tree count — running in real time within a custom-built GUI application.

01
🖼️
Image Pre-processing

Raw TIFF images are loaded, converted to grayscale, and smoothed with Gaussian blur to reduce sensor noise — preparing a clean foundation for edge analysis.

02
🔍
Tree Detection

Canny edge detection finds tree boundaries precisely. Morphological closing fills gaps; contours filtered by area (≥500px²) eliminate false positives.

03
🌲
Tree Counting

Each qualifying contour maps to a detected tree crown. A counting algorithm tallies all filtered contours and surfaces the result instantly.

04
🖥️
GUI Development

A tkinter interface lets users browse images, trigger detection, and inspect every intermediate processing stage — all with a single click.

Implementation

Test Cases

The system was tested on three distinct aerial images — from dense foliage to mixed campus scenes to bare ground — demonstrating robustness across all conditions.

ORIGINAL INPUT — Aerial drone capture, boundary trees + red soil
CONTOUR DETECTION — Green outlines mark each detected tree crown
MORPHOLOGY — Structural closing fills canopy gaps
CANNY EDGE DETECTION — Binary edge map highlighting boundaries
RESULT — 16 trees detected ✓
ORIGINAL INPUT — Mixed campus scene: building rooftop + trees + grass
CONTOUR DETECTION — Algorithm distinguishes trees from building edges
MORPHOLOGY — Closing operation applied
CANNY EDGE — Building + canopy edges mapped
RESULT — 38 trees detected ✓
ORIGINAL INPUT — Bare red-soil ground with no vegetation
CONTOUR RESULT — No contours found → no trees counted
MORPHOLOGY — Near-blank: no objects to fill
CANNY — No edges detected on bare ground
RESULT — 0 trees detected ✓ (correct!)
Algorithm Comparison

Results & Analysis

Four algorithms were compared head-to-head on real campus drone datasets. Canny Edge Detection emerged as the clear winner — here are the actual outputs side by side.

CANNY OUTPUT — Green contour outlines on original image
Canny Edge Detection
95%
Best suited for clear tree-background boundaries. Captures precise tree outlines. Handles dense foliage and mixed scenes effectively.
⭐ Best Performer — Chosen Method
Watershed Segmentation
92%
Excellent for separating closely packed trees. Detected 17 trees. Can over-segment dense canopy, slightly inflating counts.
Region-based Segmentation
88%
Strong spatial-context awareness. Detected 11 trees with red bounding boxes. Requires more compute for large images.
Adaptive Thresholding
80%
Fast and lightweight. Detected 12 trees. Struggles with complex canopy textures and mixed-background scenes.
Canny Edge Detection
95%
16 trees on TC1 · 38 on TC2 · 0 on TC3 (correct)
Watershed Segmentation
92%
17 trees detected · slight over-segmentation in dense regions
Region-based Segmentation
88%
11 trees detected · strong spatial context capture
Adaptive Thresholding
80%
12 trees detected · fastest to compute, lowest accuracy
